text: The robotic system is docked, and the surgeon performs an initial inspection of the abdomen. The hernia sac contains omentum and transverse colon, which are carefully reduced using two grasping forceps with gentle downward traction. After complete reduction, the omentum and transverse colon are inspected, showing no signs of ischemia or damage.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum in a linear fashion, followed by sharp and blunt dissection to create a preperitoneal plane.
The surgeon continues the dissection from lateral to medial to address the hernia defect, followed by the reduction of the hernia sac and preperitoneal fat.
The surgeon extends the preperitoneal plane laterally by several centimeters, creating sufficient working space. A non-absorbable suture is used to close the fascial defect in a running fashion.

The surgeon approximates the mesh centrally over the defect area using a central tagging suture. Interrupted vicryl sutures are placed circumferentially to anchor the mesh to the fascia. Once the mesh is secured, the peritoneal defect is closed with a running absorbable suture.
